task: I’m trying to tighten up how I run a small rehab from offer to close, and I want a simple checklist that catches weak spots before they turn into delays. What are the few things you’d put in place so vendors, inspections, and paperwork stay aligned without me babysitting every step?

turns.agent_response:
Here’s a simple, high-control checklist I’d use for a small rehab from offer to close, focused on preventing delays without constant babysitting.

## 1) Before you accept the offer / go under contract
- Confirm scope is realistic for budget and timeline
- Verify title, access, and any known liens/issues
- Get a rough permit check: what needs permits, what doesn’t
- Identify long-lead items early
- Build a basic draw/payment plan tied to milestones, not dates

## 2) Pre-start setup
- Written scope of work for every trade
- Bid comparison or pricing approval documented
- Start date, sequence, and inspection points mapped out
- Confirm insurance, licenses, and COI for vendors
- Order materials with lead times tracked
- Set one point of contact for each trade

## 3) Hold points before any payment
Don’t release money until these are done:
- Work for that phase is complete
- Photos taken of completed work
- Any required inspection passed
- Lien waiver received from the vendor/sub
- No open punch items for that phase, or written approval for a partial holdback

## 4) Inspection and permit control
- Keep a permit log with each required inspection
- Schedule inspections before the crew leaves a phase
- No covering work until it’s approved
- Keep permit signoff/final inspection as a hard closeout item

## 5) Paperwork control
- One folder for contract, scope, invoices, COIs, permits, waivers, photos
- Every invoice tied to a specific phase or milestone
- Change orders must be written and approved before work proceeds
- Final package required before final payment:
  - final invoice
  - final lien waiver
  - permit closeout/final signoff
  - punch list completion photos

## 6) Weekly control check
A short weekly review is usually enough:
- What’s complete?
- What’s waiting on inspection?
- What’s waiting on material?
- What’s waiting on paperwork?
- Any scope creep or extra work requests?
- Any payment blocked by open items?

## 7) The main rule that saves you money
**No payment for open work. No closeout without paperwork. No covering work before inspection.**

If you want, I can turn this into a one-page rehab control sheet or a draw checklist you can reuse on every project.

user_simulator.text:
Mostly right, but it’s a little generic. I’d tighten it around the stuff that actually blows up a rehab: change orders, permit sequencing, and payment leverage.

What I’d put in place:

1) One master tracker
- scope line item
- vendor name
- permit/inspection needed
- material lead time
- payment status
- who owes the next action

If it isn’t in the tracker, it doesn’t exist.

2) Hard gate on change orders
- no verbal extras
- no work starts on a change until price, impact, and signoff are in writing
- if they push ahead anyway, it’s their problem, not yours

3) Draws tied to proof, not dates
- photos
- inspection pass if applicable
- lien waiver
- invoice matching the scope
No proof, no money. Simple.

4) Permit/inspection calendar with buffer
- schedule inspections before the crew thinks they’re done
- don’t let work get buried behind drywall or finishes before signoff
- build slack for county lag, because it always shows up when it’s inconvenient

5) Pre-close vendor conditioning
- COI
- license check
- written scope
- start date
- penalty if they miss critical sequencing
- one person responsible on their side

6) Weekly red-flag review
Only three questions:
- what’s late?
- what’s hidden?
- what can stop the next draw?

That’s the real control point.

If you want this usable, I’d turn it into a one-page checklist with columns for owner / due date / status / block. Otherwise it’s just a nice list and those don’t save money.

turns.agent_response:
Yes — that’s the right tighter frame.

If I were trying to keep a small rehab moving without babysitting, I’d only put 5 controls in place:

## 1) One master tracker
Keep every trade, permit, and action item in one sheet with these columns:
- scope line item
- vendor
- permit / inspection needed
- material lead time
- next action
- owner
- due date
- payment status
- block / risk

If it’s not in the tracker, it doesn’t count.

## 2) Change orders are a hard gate
This is the big one.
- no verbal extras
- no work starts until price, scope impact, and schedule impact are written and approved
- no “we’ll sort it out later”
- if they proceed without approval, that cost sits with them until you decide otherwise

That keeps people from burying extras into the draw or calling them “closeout.”

## 3) Draws require proof
No proof, no money.
Each draw should require:
- photos
- invoice tied to the actual scope
- inspection pass if that phase needs one
- lien waiver
- confirmation there are no open punch items for that phase

This preserves leverage and keeps payment tied to progress, not promises.

## 4) Permit and inspection sequencing
Build the sequence before work starts:
- demo
- rough-in
- rough inspection
- close walls
- finishes
- final inspection
- closeout

And make inspections hard gates:
- no covering work before approval
- schedule inspections early, not when the crew is “basically done”
- leave buffer for county delays

A lot of rehab pain comes from work getting buried before it’s signed off.

## 5) Weekly red-flag review
Keep it short:
- what is late?
- what is hidden?
- what can stop the next draw?
- what is waiting on someone else?
- what extra work is being hinted at but not approved?

That’s enough to catch most problems before they stall the job.

## The simplest rule set
- If it isn’t written, it isn’t approved
- If it isn’t inspected, it isn’t covered
- If it isn’t documented, it doesn’t get paid

If you want, I can turn this into a one-page rehab control sheet with columns and checkboxes you can reuse on every deal.

user_simulator.text:
Yes. That’s the useful version.

The only thing I’d add is a real escalation rule, because that’s where jobs drift:

- if a vendor misses a sequencing deadline, they get one written warning
- if it affects the critical path, you freeze the next draw until they recover it
- if they start freelancing scope, they don’t get to argue it into the invoice later

And I’d keep a separate permit log from the vendor tracker. Different problem, different pressure point. County delays and contractor delays are not the same thing, and mixing them makes people sloppy.

Otherwise, this is the right structure. The tracker, change-order gate, proof-based draws, inspection sequencing, weekly red-flag review — that’s the core. Everything else is just noise.
